title: Heatwave grips State, water scarcity worsens across districts - The Hans India
full_text:
Karnataka is reeling under an intense heatwave, with temperatures rising steadily across the state and triggering widespread distress. From Belagavi in the north to Chamarajanagar in the south, the scorching conditions have disrupted daily life, strained water resources, and raised serious health concerns.
Northern districts such as Yadgir, Kalaburagi, and Raichur are among the worst affected, with residents struggling to cope with extreme temperatures. In Yadgir, authorities have installed ‘green nets’ at busy traffic junctions like Subhash Circle to provide shade for commuters waiting at signals. The temporary measure has offered some relief to two-wheeler riders exposed to direct sunlight.
In Kalaburagi, people are increasingly turning to swimming pools to escape the heat, highlighting the severity of the situation. Meanwhile, health experts in Raichur have reported a rise in heat-related illnesses, particularly among infants. Cases of dehydration, vomiting, and diarrhoea have increased, prompting doctors to advise parents to keep children indoors between noon and 4 pm.
The impact of the heatwave is also visible in Kolar, where a dedicated 10-bed ward has been set up at the district hospital to treat heatstroke patients. In rural and forested regions of Chamarajanagar and Chikkaballapur, rising temperatures have forced snakes out of their habitats, creating additional safety concerns for residents.
Water scarcity has compounded the crisis. In Belagavi district’s Chikodi taluk, women in Neralagudda village are reportedly walking up to 2 kilometres daily to fetch drinking water. In Dharwad district’s Kundgol area, nearly 300 households depend on a single tap, with residents waiting for hours to collect a pot of water.
The situation is equally grim in coastal Udupi, where villagers in Byndoor are forced to cross the Souparnika river by boat just to access drinking water. Reservoir levels are also depleting rapidly. The Tungabhadra Dam currently holds only around 12 TMC of water, raising concerns for districts like Koppal, Ballari, Raichur, and Vijayanagara.
The crisis is not limited to humans. Wildlife is also suffering due to water shortages. In Chamarajanagar, herds of elephants have been spotted migrating towards water sources such as the Palar stream in search of relief. With forecasts indicating continued high temperatures, authorities are under pressure to implement urgent measures to ensure water supply, protect public health, and prevent further escalation of the crisis.
